topic: International treaty examination of the NZ – UAE Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, and Agreement between the Government of New Zealand...
description: Thursday 20 February 2025 | Foreign Affairs, Defence and Trade Committee International treaty examination of the NZ – UAE Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, and Agreement between the Government of New Zealand and the Government of the United Arab Emirates on the Promotion and Protection of Investments Submitters Nga Toki Whakarururanga - Maui Solomon Pirirakau Tribal Authority - Incorporated - Jason Ake NZ International Business Forum - Stephen Jacobi, Executive Director Jane Kelsey
descriptionSummary: International treaty examination of the NZ – UAE Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, and Agreement between the Government of New Zealand.... International treaty examination of the NZ – UAE Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement, and Agreement between the Government of New Zealand...

chunks.text: Okay. Believe we are low screening now. Yep. Okay. And we'll be joined online by our all of our submitters today. All first submitter. Uh, sorry. Just to confirm, for anyone watching online, we are now commencing hearings on the International Treaty examination of the New Zealand United Arab Emirates Close Economic Partnership Agreement.

Uh, we have received submissions on this, and we are hearing from submitters today. The first of those is Mr. Solomon. Welcome back for the committee. Thank you. We have 10 minutes sit down. We've seen your submission and we welcome you. Your, uh, speaking to that, uh, and any questions if this time? Yeah, thank you.

Oh, rung or Ma Solomon. Um, I'm a UMA to Naga under our mediation agreement with the Crown Naga, uh, is meant to have genuine and meaningful influence on negotiations From a Ang perspective, uh, that proved impossible with this agreement. For example, the final draft of the U-A-E-F-T-A texts were provided to us under strict confidentiality agreement on 13 September, 2024, uh, with a request, request to respond within a week after providing extensive

and considered input to MFA on 19 December, we were advised that the agreement would be concluded only five days later. This is not genuine and meaningful engagement as required under our mediation agreement, we're also mandated to provide the three T impact assessments of agreements as set out at the end of the crown's.

Um, NIA, uh, we note that there was less opportunity for influence on the GGCC negotiations because the core techs were negotiated almost two decades ago, and additional texts were rushed through in parallel to the UAE. We were critical of a non-paper on Maori indigenous trades that the Crown provided to the UAE that purported to explain to GY and just justify various negotiation proposals.

This paper seriously misrepresented to, uh, to treat by giving an incorrect impression that Maori had relinquished their sovereignty and that T Treaty was only concerned with protection of property rights. This is not the case. Our submission highlights a wide range of areas that can't be covered in the allotted, um, time of 10 minutes.

The time given to our submission is highly unsatisfactory, and we request that in future submissions that are more democratic and respectful, time is allocated to hearings, oral submissions. Given these time constraints, I'll refer to five key issues, but that doesn't mean the others aren't all important.

Also, firstly, indigenous people's economic and trade cooperation. Chapter 15, NATO engaged most intensively with this chapter. Uh, but despite that input, this chapter follows the patent of recent agreements. It lacks any substantive commitments by the parties, is unenforceable and has no impact on the rest of the agreement.

It is impossible from the information currently available to assess what genuinely new commercial benefits Maori exporters may achieve from the market access provisions. But we note that the NIA says exporters already received benefits, uh, of a unilateral UAE duty exemption program.

So we cannot, we cannot see how Maori firms, especially many SMEs, are meant to gain from this. Secondly, the Treaty of Wang exception, the Crown continues to misrepresent, uh, the Treaty of Wang, exception, exception in its NOA as protecting its policy space, space to meet its obligations under Tia Wang.

However, it well knows this is not the case. The exception only applies to measures adopted by the Crown to give quote, more favorable treatment unquote, to Maori and is subject to additional conditions. It would also not protect measures that are designed to benefit Maori and other communities such, such as social procurement programs.

The mediation agreement promised to revisit this exception, which the Crown first devised way back in 2001, but MFA keeps rolling it over in spite of our objections. Three intellectual property rights. This chapter reinforces the Western intellectual property model that Maori challenged in the Y 2 6 2 inquiry and elsewhere, and which has no protections for the exercise of Ranga ma Maori or kaki responsibilities over tga.

For example, the provision on geographical indications provides no recognition of Maori terms such as Manuka. Instead, the sharing of information on promoting and protecting Manuka honey is listed as one possible error area of cooperation. An Article 15.4 of the Unenforceable Indigenous People's Economic Trade and Cooperation chapter, the ip, uh, chapter's provision on genetic resources, traditional knowledge and cultural expressions.

Article 13.35 is equally weak. The UAE and ult. New Zealands may establish, quote, appropriate measures, unquote, for their protection, but only where the domestic laws and other international obligations allow. They should also quote, endeavor unquote, to create a database or digital library for such information, a move that carries real dangers for Maori unless they're also, uh, exercise their Rana or control over that information for the climate crisis.

Article 14.7 on climate change recognizes the unique connection, uh, of indigenous peoples to the environment, the right to protect and develop their systems of knowledge, practices, and values, and the valuable contribution indigenous knowledge can make towards sustainable trade and investments, including solutions to climate change.

Yet the article does not give a effect to that. Instead, it promotes the kind of financial and technological solutions that Naau has cited in the WA tribunal's Climate Change Inquiry is antithetical to Maori values, knowledge and practices, and is designed to avoid changing corporate and national practices that significantly contribute to the climate crisis.

Finally, five, digital Trade. The Crown recognizes its treaty obligations and digital trade chapters following the WA Tangi Tribunal finding that the TPPA chapter breached its treaty obligations. In this agreement, it has successfully included protections for measure to meet those obligations, providing strong protections than in the EU FTA.

And this should be used as a baseline by the Crown in the future. Unfortunately, the Crown has not addressed concerns about a less fail safe protection from Maori data sovereignty and digital governance in the schedule to the Trade and services chapter. The digital trade, uh, chapter is enforceable. So a commitment to cooperate on digital inclusion has the potential to provide positive benefits to Maori business, especially Maori digital innovators.

Conclusion throughout its input on these negotiations, NAOKI has emphasized the right of Maori under WGI in the United Nations declaration on the rights of indigenous peoples to self-determination and to participate in decision making on matters that affect them through representatives chosen by themselves in accordance with their own procedures that never occurred now recognizes that officials were themselves under intense pressure in these negotiations.

However, the process and substance of these negotiations have once again, subordinated to tgi to other priorities determined unilaterally by the Crown breach of its Titi obligations. That's the end of my submission. Excellent. Any questions?

Right? Do we have any questions for Mr. Solomon? First question from Dr. Lauren Hunan. Thank you, chair. Um, thank you so much Maori for your submission. I think one of the things that we are interested, uh, okay. I am interested in, um, when we ask MFA about Maori engagement, you know, they talked about the gains that have made in this and how they have to negotiate the differences between cultural differences between, like you said, GCC and UAE and ot.

Um, from your perspective, hypothetically speaking, if NA to was part of that negotiation, how would you have negotiated differently to ensure better protection for or better upholding of utility, but also better protection from an intellectual property perspective? Mm-hmm. Thank you for that.

Um, so Nataki has been advocating indeed, even in the Y 2 62 claim going back 20, 30 years, we've been saying that the Maori voice, we need to be sitting at the table, um, so that we can convey those issues and our concerns directly, um, to the, so that they, the party on the other side of the table, the officials on the other side of the table can fully understand what our concerns are.

And I think if we were able to do that, um, ka um, we would achieve better outcomes. Um, but, uh, really our voice is excluded from that process. Just a supplementary question, I think one of the, um, again, we asked this question last week to Amfa.

They did have someone there and you know, pardon my ignorance, they did have someone there, um, who is, who did express how he spent some time there and is working with the people when they were at UAE on negotiating some of these, and particularly from like a Maori perspective, I guess, um, my question is around like, do you think that the, the voice that is at the decision making table is not sufficient or that, although they may have someone there who is, um, of my background, but the the actual engagement itself is still lacking?

Mm-hmm. So, I, I wanna just kind of get a sense from No, No, I, I understand the question. Thank you. 'cause it is an important question. And look, we, we have no doubt that, um, crown, you know, Maori who work for the Crown are doing the very best that they can. Mm-hmm. But they're giving largely still a Crown perspective.

Um, NAA is, uh, a nationally mandated body, the only nationally mandated body in this particular trade space, international trade space. And so we want to, we, we require to have unfiltered, um, and have an independent voice at the table so that our concerns can be directly, uh, articulated and, uh, um, our perspectives put, uh, unfiltered through a crown process.

Well unfiltered through, um, uh, another voice, if you will. That's really helpful. Thank you. Very good. All right. Thank you very much for your time this morning. Appreciate your submission on the NZ Thank you. Economic partnership agreement. Thank you. Thank you very much. Me. All right. We move now to our next submitter, uh, tribal Authority Incorporated.

just going, going to go cut to the chase 'cause I know everybody's, um, very busy. We all know how that's going at the moment with this government. a huge issue for us indigenous biodiversity. When we, um, look at the harbors, for example, the amount of stuff that comes in, um, to our harbor, uh, from overseas, um, is incredibly, um, damaging. I'll go back to the point I raised earlier. I really want to pick up on a rather theme that both in your submission in, I took for KA on the submission, it is around intellectual property and benefits to SMEs. Um, what do you think, because I accept that, you know, from both, um, your perspective and, uh, ND W's perspective that it's not sufficient, but what do you think is the tangible harm or harmful outcome it will result from if we continuing having trade agreements that does not take intellectual properties and SMEs or ipu or Maori SMEs into account?

Yeah, so I mean, that, that's a very good question. Um, as we know, uh, as, um, as Maori, as Iwi, um, have been engaged in international trade since the, since the arrival of, of, of the settlers to this country. And so we do have an historical understanding of the importance of trade, um, not just, um, in, in inside domestically, but also, um, externally in order for us to get back to that, um, to that place where we are trading successfully and our interests are being acknowledged in a way that represents a, uh, a uniquely indigenous Maori voice

that requires a conversation, well, more than a conversation actually, it requires a very, very, um, purposeful, um, conversation with Iwi with Maori. And it's not as if we don't have the mechanisms for that to take place, because we do the Iwi Chairs forum has, uh, numbers there. Uh, Maui's group also has very good, um, uh, acute, uh, sort of, um, insights into this, um, Copa, um, insofar as it relates to, um, uh, the effect of, uh, international trade agreements on Iwi and Maori.

We, what I'm trying to say is that we act, we have been acting in this space for a long time in the trade space. SMEs, um, to, for example, Kiwi fruit is, is we've got a bunch of, um, uh, Maori, um, kiwi fruit owners, um, in the, in the primary producing sector.

Um, you've got fomo, which represents a, a large number, um, of Maori primary producers as well. So that's what I'm saying. Um, we, we do have a huge footprint in the space, own 10% of the fishery, um, resource, um, a a large number of the dairy resource, uh, forestry, for example.

Um, so it behooves, I think, uh, the, the crown, the government to engage, um, with those bodies. They're easy to find. Okay, thank you. Uh, we've got time for a question from the honorable penny. He, Uh, I just quickly on the paka recommendations about the removal of indigenous biodiversity, at what point and in time do you think then if that was to happen, uh, that the biodiversity would be back on the table given the biodiversity is very different across South Island, And, and that was the key when I was involved

with pulling together the conversations, penny. Um, there were key fundamental differences, even in the honey industry, um, between the, um, honey that's grown in the north, um, the honey that's grown on the east coast of, uh, of the north island. They are distinctly different varieties, um, of, um, of produce.

Um, and they have different characteristics, um, as well. So biodiversity formed a key part of the, um, the, the plant variety rights, um, system as well that, um, I think 21, um, provisions, uh, provided for to give us, you know, a lot more assurity around that.

It's a little bit of a shame that, you know, some of our rights are more recognized from international bodies rather than from our own own ones here. Very good. All right. Thank you very much for your submission this morning. Appreciate it. Right. We'll move on to our next submitter.
Our next submission will be from the NZ International Business Forum.

Good morning, Steven. Good morning. Good morning. Excellent. All right. Welcome along to the committee this morning. We look forward to hearing your submission and support of your written submission, which we've received on the NZUA epa. We have 10 minutes set down for you this morning. Thank you. Uh, um,

Koto Tato, I seem to be bathed in sunshine here. I'm in the sunny, um, Hawke's Bay tomato Maui, where the sun always shines. Uh, and it's a great pleasure, uh, to join you today and thank you chair for this opportunity. I'm joining you on behalf of the members of the New Zealand International Business Forum and Export New Zealand, which is, as you know, a division of business New Zealand.

I serve as the executive director of the New Zealand International Business Forum. I'm an advisory board member of Export New Zealand. I'm sorry, my colleague Josh Tan, the executive director of Export New Zealand, can't join us today, but he has entrusted me with this, uh, task to deliver, uh, these comments. As you'll have seen, our submission is a joint one, uh, and between us, we represent New Zealand exporters, large, small, and medium sized, all across Al Tua.

We are strongly in support of ratification of New Zealand's latest FTA, the SEPA with the United Arab Emirates colleagues. This is a high quality, comprehensive, and ambitious agreement, the negotiation of which has been concluded in record time, and we are grateful to trade Minister McClay and to his officials for the excellent work.

As our submission notes, this agreement will provide comprehensive tariff elimination within a rapid timeframe timeframe. Now, currently applied tariffs in the UAE against New Zealand are not high, uh, but there is with this agreement and opportunity to grow further New Zealand exporters, uh, exports, especially in the dairy, red meat, seafood, horticulture, high value foods, and agritech sectors.

And it should likewise create new opportunity for UAE goods in our own market and for services and through the companion bilateral investment treaty, greater flows of foreign direct investment into our market. In terms of the trade rules the agreement provides, the CPA provides all the elements we would normally want to see from a high quality agreement, very much along the lines of New Zealand's, other high quality agreements, particularly C-P-T-P-P and the New Zealand UK FTA.

And that includes provisions, uh, designed to ensure that trade is both sustainable and inclusive. And we are pleased to see that the right of governments to regulate for legitimate public policy reasons and the ability of the government to meet the crown's obligations to Maori have been safeguarded.

As you will know, the SIPPA has been instrumental also in securing, uh, a long sought after FDA with the members of the wider Gulf Cooperation Council, and we look forward to this agreement coming before the committee for ratification. Now, you may have noted that in paragraph 28 of our submission, we welcome the government's provisions aimed at delivering end-to-end, secure and trusted paperless trade.

These provisions are largely aspirational in nature, and the chapter is not enforceable. Um, it they largely mirror other undertakings in other trade agreements. New Zealand has entered into paperless trade is a key interest for us. Paperless trade refers to replacing the vast amounts of paper that move around the world, a company and goods as they move through supply chains.

Replacing paper with the trusted exchange of data can reduce costs, improve security, and provide information for regulators and consumers alike. It's the sort of thing New Zealand can and should be doing as part of a packet package of measures to double export value.

Unfortunately, I have to say that it remains a disappointment for both our organizations that success of New Zealand governments have so far failed to take the steps necessary to towards devising a national strategy, uh, to implement paperless trade. Sutter's strategy is not without complication, it requires legislative change, engagement with trade partners and boosting the capability of businesses.

We urge the government now to follow through on the various undertakings to implement paperless trade as a number of our trading partners are doing, such as Singapore, the uk, and Australia. Not to do so could leave New Zealand behind in the international trading space chair.

Members of the committee, uh, the New Zealand UAE SEPA may not be a transformational agreement in terms of its impact on our economy. That's because the market is largely open already, but it is a very good agreement, and I think one we can be proud of that with your endorsement.

The SEPA will come into effect at a time when we are facing a sharp increase in protectionism in the form of tariffs threatened or otherwise. And other trade barriers around the world is very significant indeed. It shows that there are still economies who believe in the values of openness, effective trade rules, and economic integration.

And for the reasons set out in our submission, we recommend that the committee indicate its support for the prompt ratification of the New Zealand United Arab Emirates. Uh, SEPA ate. Excellent. Thank you very much for your submission this morning. I just wanted to pick up on a couple of points under 16, you note that UA E is consuming 12% more food each year, which I think is an impressive growth rate, but that our exports have been static.

So I guess that highlights the potential opportunity there as well. Um, but really interested if you could just delve a bit more into, uh, what you've highlighted under point 30 in relation to the investor state dispute settlement, uh, and your disappointment that we have not included that aspect. Yes. Well, thank you for that. Um, just in relation to your first comment, I think that this issue around, um, you know, being able to link up with the uae, you know, food strategies extremely important and it shows why this agreement is going to be necessary above the, the sort of tiff treatment we face at the moment, including for securing our trade in the long term.

On the question of investor state dispute settlement, I mean, this is something on which we don't agree with the government, neither this one, uh, or the last one. Uh, so there you go. We are really, are really an independent body. I mean, our thought about this, and this is a very, uh, controversial area, uh, I, I agree, but our thought about this is that investment protections are required, uh, to protect investors against, you know, the non-legitimate action of governments in related relation to their investments, in particular, their,

their private property. You know, it's not for a moment that we are suggesting that any New Zealand government would act like this, but there are lots of instances in other governments acting like this around the world that could and on occasion, but not very often affect New Zealand's investments overseas. So we think investor state dispute settlement is simply a part and parcel of the normal things you would include in an investment treaty to stimulate, promote, uh, further foreign direct investment.

Very good. Alright, Dr. Laurens, may I ask a supplementary question on top of what you ask or you left, but Yep. Okay. Far away. Um, very quickly, again, let's just, uh, supplementary question on that. Um, what's your view of the potentiality that if we do have something like ISDS that investors can hold a government ransom on certain decisions as we see with the Clive Palmer lawsuit of Australian government for $420 billion?

How do you mitigate that? Uh, you mitigate it by devising ISDS, um, uh, provisions that provide the continuing ability of governments to regulate in the national interest that exclude certain areas, uh, that are, um, deemed to be, you know, of sufficient weight, that those protections don't apply.

And it's all a matter about how these things are, are constructed. And there has been quite an evolution in the development of ISDS procedures in overseas jurisdictions where we've seen, uh, the sort of, um, instances that you reference. Um, these generally, you know, date backed quite a long period of time. Unfortunately, New Zealand hasn't been for some time now in the business of including these arrangements, so we would be starting all over again.

I mean, this is a matter of, um, I I fully understand, um, some controversy and controversy and requiring discussion, but nevertheless, not to include, um, investment protections detract somewhat from the sort of environment for foreign direct investment that we are trying to create here in Alua.

I have time to ask my question very quickly. Okay, go for it. Okay. Um, thank you for your submission. And you mentioned something, you mentioned digital economy as one of them, as we know that, you know, often government legislation policies do not move as fast as large tech companies, and particularly when we see the continued politicization of the tech industry between let's say US China, how do you sort of deal with the tension of us having, and you mentioned Singapore, how do you deal with the tension of the politicization

of tech in our digital commerce and the protection of data because of how it could be used by large tech giants? That, that is a very good question, and I think it is at the forefront, uh, of negotiators concerns when they're negotiating these digital economy, uh, chapters. That's why, largely speaking, they're not enforceable and are aspirational, uh, in, in nature in the context of a, of a trade agreement I'm talking about.

Uh, and, um, you know, again, I think it's about, uh, making sure that we are ahead of the game when it comes to devising digital economy, uh, provisions. You'll know that New Zealand is a founder member of the Digital Economy Partnership Agreement, uh, which is, um, designed precisely in many respects to address that particular problem.

It includes a whole, uh, series of, um, steps designed to work with our trading partners to divine, to devise rules that are fit for the current environment. But you're right, this is a fast moving, uh, area. Our interest here relates mostly to, um, and I'm talking about our exporters interest relates mostly to paperless trade, which we really wish the New Zealand government would get a move on, uh, with, um, putting in place and the risk not to doing so, as I said is that we just, uh, fall behind.

So, uh, this is an area that I think warrants a lot, uh, you know, further attention and consideration. Thank you. Excellent. All right. Thank you very much for your time this morning, Mr. Jacob. Be, we, we have heard loud and clear your views on the paperless trade. Thank you for taking the time to indeed for us. Indeed. Appreciate it.

Yo, thank you very much. Thank you. Okay, members, we'll move to our final submission for the morning. We joined online by Jane Kelsey. Morning, professor Kelsey, how are you? Excellent. We've received your submission. You can take that as read.

Uh, we have 10 minutes allocated for your submission today, and there may be questions if you wish to leave time for that. Thank you. Uh, ki chair. Well, as you will know from the letters that you've received, I do not accept 10 minutes is the limit on my submission, and I give a heads up, uh, to the committee that the ongoing refusal to take the International Treaty examination as seriously as it needs to be, is about to be escalated into a much bigger discussion.

Um, I took the advantage of this submission on the UAE, uh, to remind the committee that in making submissions as I have done for 30 plus years of my international standing to analyze the agreement and to challenge the national interest analysis prepared by the Ministry of Foreign Affairs and Trade to justify the agreement that NIA as my submission shows, uh, is misleading in a number of places, particularly pertinent to my expertise in this instance, is that,

um, I was invited by, uh, Dr. Kearney, the minister of, uh, state for foreign trade from the United Arab Emirates, who was also responsible for, uh, this agreement from the UAE side to be one of six people, the others, including a former director general of the World Trade Organization to advise Dr.

Tanni on the UA e's hosting of the WTO Ministerial Conference last year. And I was invited to attend that conference as his guest. So this is not just a busy body academic seeking to throw a spanner in the works. I have deep knowledge and recognized international expertise in this area that the committee, um, needs to pay more heed to because MFAs NIA is deeply problematic in relation to this agreement.

Um, I've been particularly aware, uh, that both the UAE and the New Zealand Minister of Trade have set as objectives securing as many agreements as possible as quickly as possible. The agreements therefore become a matter of optics rather than a matter of substance.

And that is especially evident if we look at a number of core aspects of, um, how MFA seeks to justify the agreement. If we look at the NIA, we see the dilemmas when the prescriptions are largely uncontested. Now, as, as Mai Solomon said, yes, uh, there was a treaty assessment from that is printed at the end of the NIA,

but that is not in any way influencing MFA's thinking about this. Part of the problem with this agreement was that not only when negotiations conducted in secret, there were cursory public consultations, but those of us who did see the text in that three month period and have an opportunity to input back on it under strict confidentiality in my case as an advisor to we're not in fact able to take those issues out to get advice and feedback into the process.

And it is therefore not a process in itself that can carry social license and legitimacy. But if we look at some of the details of it, we, we see some of the consequences of MFA writing the, the narrative. And it was interesting that my old friend, Stephen Jacoby, even admitted that there was very little in this agreement commercially, uh, for them.

So if we are looking at the, the current 2023 level of trade, uh, with the UAE, we are looking at 1.1% of New Zealand's global exports. If we are looking at the quantum overall as MFA highlights of New Zealand's FTAs that will now be covered, um, sorry, new Zealand's exports that will now be covered by FTAs, that carries with it an implication that FTAs somehow contribute to economic growth and export growth.

Yet if we look at the data, and I've been looking at the OECD data on this, new Zealand's exports have actually fallen as a proportion of GDP over recent years as we've been accumulating FTAs. There is no direct correlation, it's there as implied in the NIA.

Moreover, the areas that are identified as of interest in relation to the UAE such as areas of of red meat and dairy are areas where we are already largely at saturation. We are not gonna be expanding domestic production of those. So that means that whatever might end up going to the UAE is gonna be trade diversion, not trade creation.

If we go beyond that, the notion of tariff savings here that could be 42.6 million is misleading in terms of what those so-called tariff savings actually mean. It's not money in the pocket of exporters. It's potentially lower prices in the UAE if the tariff cuts are in fact asked on to consumers.

And if consumers choice leads them to buy more of New Zealand's exports, yet, not in the summary, but down in page 84 of the NIA, we see the concession that in fact the majority of New Zealand's exports to the UAE are actually under a a duty exemption program anyway.

So we've got a lot of smokes, smoke and mirrors going on here. We need to start looking much more distinctively at what these agreements actually do and do not do, how much resource we are putting into them for what, and I note that, for example, in the government procurement area, the UAE has has, uh, reserved the right of a 10% premium to advantage.

Its SMEs. New Zealand doesn't have any equivalent of that, that might actually provide some potential new opportunities in the procurement area if we actually know what purchasing might be involved with the UAE. And we don't have that empirical data. But if we are looking, say at Maori enterprises, Maori enterprises, uh, are predominantly SMEs, why can't we have similar kinds of, um, advantages in our procurement?

Largely, I suspect because we've already given away the right to do that in the WTO gp, um, area, let me talk about, uh, investment and services. Um, as one who has a, a long history of working on investor state dispute settlement, unlike Mr.

Jacoby, I'm extremely pleased to see this government continuing the position of no ISDS and new agreements. But when we are looking at, at the realities of ISDS, now, it is in fact a a a pariah, uh, in many international agreements. And they are designed exactly as, uh, uh, as the green party, uh, members said, uh, to chill governments by threatening to bring very expensive disputes against governments if they proceed with those policies in other areas. In, um, the investment chapter, it is disappointing to see us continuing, uh, to celebrate the restrictions on so-called performance requirements.

We call them trade related investment measures in, in the trade context. Shane Jones encountered this problem, uh, when he was trying to get, um, more milling done in in some of the, um, uh, mills especially, and, and FAT said, you got a problem there. We've got issues as well in the services chapter. Um, I raised the question around water and water bottling.
